It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

- World model: Clicking the red dot at (34,10) only changed the HUD bar (row 63), not any gameplay objects. This was a wasted action. The board has: (1) a green container with red dot (top), (2) a yellow bar with red dot (left), (3) a green double-container with two N U-shapes (middle-right), (4) a yellow double-container with two Y U-shapes (bottom-middle), (5) two red cross markers (top-right, bottom-left). The goal likely involves moving items between containers or matching colors.
- Plan: Try clicking on other elements - the red cross markers, the U-shapes, or the container walls to find what actually changes gameplay.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.

Level 1: you have used 34 actions; a strong score needs about 20 or fewer. You are 1.7x over the target.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: Again `no_impact: true` with `total_cells_changed: 2`. The harness keeps saying no impact but cells ARE changing. Let me check what actually changed this time.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check what changed between previous and current frame
if last_transition:
    before = last_transition.before_frame.segmentation
    after = last_transition.after_frame.segmentation
    
    # Compare N/Y objects
    print("=== Before ===")
    for node in before['nodes']:
        if node['color'] in ['N', 'Y', 'r']:
            print(f"  {node['color']}: pixels={node['pixels']}, hash={node['hash']}")
    
    print("\n=== After ===")
    for node in after['nodes']:
        if node['color'] in ['N', 'Y', 'r']:
            print(f"  {node['color']}: pixels={node['pixels']}, hash={node['hash']}")
    
    # Check what cells changed using ascii
    before_lines = last_transition.before_frame.ascii.split('\n')
    after_lines = last_transition.after_frame.ascii.split('\n')
    for i in range(len(before_lines)):
        if before_lines[i] != after_lines[i]:
            print(f"\nRow {i} changed:")
            print(f"  Before: {before_lines[i]}")
            print(f"  After:  {after_lines[i]}")
            # Find differing cols
            for j in range(len(before_lines[i])):
                if before_lines[i][j] != after_lines[i][j]:
                    print(f"    Col {j}: {before_lines[i][j]} -> {after_lines[i][j]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
OK so the ONLY change is on row 63, which is the HUD bar. The containers did NOT change at all! So clicking on (10, 27), the G frame cell, had no effect on gameplay objects.
So the harness is CORRECT that this was no impact. The container sizes are still (5, 5), same as before.
Now I need to reconsider everything. The harness says ALL actions have `gameplay_changed: false`. But earlier I saw container sizes change! Let me re-examine the history to find when containers actually changed.
Wait, let me re-read the history. In the earlier turns, I was tracking container sizes based on segmentation. But maybe I was wrong - maybe the containers never actually changed, and I was misinterpreting the segmentation.
Actually, looking at the initial frame segmentation:
- N: pixels=14, hash=4a8b1bf478376ae7 (green single container)
- N: pixels=7, hash=4456667cdb9e8032 (N U-shape 1)
- N: pixels=7, hash=7334edbf88590890 (N U-shape 2)
- Y: pixels=23, hash=b2044a421f7ab0b8 (yellow single container)
- Y: pixels=7, hash=9145a97e3f9f276b (Y U-shape 1)
- Y: pixels=7, hash=ceacc215e8c95ea1 (Y U-shape 2)
And the current frame:
- N: pixels=5, hash=4d0e5afd82382971 (green single container)
- N: pixels=7, hash=4456667cdb9e8032 (N U-shape 1)
- N: pixels=7, hash=7334edbf88590890 (N U-shape 2)
- Y: pixels=5, hash=ffd5a52c168b2181 (yellow single container)
- Y: pixels=7, hash=9145a97e3f9f276b (Y U-shape 1)
- Y: pixels=7, hash=ceacc215e8c95ea1 (Y U-shape 2)
The hashes are DIFFERENT for the single containers! Initial N hash=4a8b1bf478376ae7, current N hash=4d0e5afd82382971. Initial Y hash=b2044a421f7ab0b8, current Y hash=ffd5a52c168b2181.
So the containers HAVE changed. But the harness says `gameplay_changed: false`. This means the harness is wrong about what constitutes gameplay.
Wait, but the last action (MOUSE 10, 27) only changed row 63. So the containers didn't change on THIS action. But they DID change on earlier actions.
Let me check a specific earlier transition where I thought containers changed. Let me look at history[3] or history[4].

<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me check an earlier transition where I thought containers changed
# Let me look at history[3] which was MOUSE(20, 39)
if len(history) > 3:
    h = history[3]
    print(f"History {3}: action={h.action}")
    seg_before = history[2].frame.segmentation if len(history) > 2 else None
    seg_after = h.frame.segmentation
    
    if seg_before:
        print("\n=== Before (history[2]) ===")
        for node in seg_before['nodes']:
            if node['color'] in ['N', 'Y']:
                print(f"  {node['color']}: pixels={node['pixels']}, hash={node['hash']}")
    
    print("\n=== After (history[3]) ===")
    for node in seg_after['nodes']:
        if node['color'] in ['N', 'Y']:
            print(f"  {node['color']}: pixels={node['pixels']}, hash={node['hash']}")

# Also check the result of history[3]
if len(transitions) > 3:
    t = transitions[3]
    print(f"\nTransition {3} result: gameplay_changed={t.result.get('gameplay_changed')}, no_impact={t.result.get('no_impact')}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
